question: What is the correct difference between document.getElementById() and document.querySelector()?
options: getElementById() uses CSS selectors, while querySelector() only uses IDs
getElementById() searches only by ID, while querySelector() uses CSS selectors
Both methods work in exactly the same way
querySelector() returns multiple elements, while getElementById() returns only one
explanation: getElementById() searches for elements only by their id attribute and takes a string as its argument. querySelector() uses CSS selector syntax, allowing for more flexible searches. querySelector() returns only the first matching element; querySelectorAll() is the method that returns multiple elements.

question: What is the correct difference between innerHTML and textContent?
options: textContent parses and renders HTML tags
innerHTML is always safe from a security perspective
innerHTML parses HTML, while textContent treats content as plain text
Both properties work identically
explanation: innerHTML parses strings as HTML and inserts them into the DOM, which can be vulnerable to XSS attacks. textContent treats strings as plain text, so HTML tags are displayed as literal text. When inserting user input into the DOM, using textContent is the safer choice.

question: After running the following code, how many child elements does #container have?
explanation:
innerHTML = '
A
' replaces the existing content and creates one div element. Then appendChild(span) adds a span element, resulting in a total of 2 child elements (div and span). innerHTML removes all existing children and replaces them with new content.

question: What is a correct advantage of Event Delegation?
options: It is more memory-efficient than attaching individual event listeners to each child element
It can prevent event bubbling
It only works during the capturing phase
It cannot be applied to dynamically added elements
explanation: Event delegation is a pattern where a single event listener is registered on a parent element, and event bubbling is leveraged to handle events from child elements. It reduces memory usage and automatically applies to dynamically added child elements. The actual clicked element is identified using event.target.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options: active, visible
active, hidden
hidden
visible, hidden
explanation: classList.add('active', 'visible') adds both classes. classList.toggle('active') removes 'active' (since it already exists). classList.replace('visible', 'hidden') replaces 'visible' with 'hidden'. The final result is only 'hidden'.

question: What is the main reason for using DocumentFragment?
options: Because it cannot be inserted directly into the DOM tree
Because it is slower than innerHTML but safer
Because it is automatically garbage collected from memory
To minimize reflows by adding multiple nodes at once
explanation: DocumentFragment is a lightweight in-memory DOM. You can add multiple nodes to the Fragment first, then insert them all into the actual DOM at once. This prevents the repeated reflows and repaints that would occur when adding each node individually, improving performance.

question: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: querySelectorAll() returns a static NodeList. It is a snapshot taken at the time of the call, so subsequent DOM changes are not reflected. Therefore, items.length remains 2. On the other hand, children is a live HTMLCollection, so after removing li1, it becomes 1.

question: What is the correct way to set an attribute on a DOM element?
options: element.attr('name', 'value')
element.attribute('name', 'value')
element.setProperty('name', 'value')
element.setAttribute('name', 'value')
explanation: setAttribute() is the standard DOM API for setting HTML attributes on an element. If the attribute already exists, the value is updated; if not, a new attribute is created. Use getAttribute() to read an attribute and removeAttribute() to remove one.

question: What is the correct difference between append() and appendChild()?
options: appendChild() can also insert strings
append() can insert multiple nodes and strings at the same time
append() returns the inserted node
appendChild() can insert multiple nodes at the same time
explanation: append() can accept multiple Node objects and strings (DOMStrings) as arguments simultaneously, and has no return value. appendChild() accepts only a single Node as an argument and returns the inserted node. append() is a more modern API that offers greater flexibility.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options: undefined
null
Alice
TypeError
explanation: The dataset API accesses data-* attributes using camelCase. dataset.userName is converted to the data-user-name attribute in the actual HTML. Therefore, getAttribute('data-user-name') retrieves the value 'Alice'. The camelCase to kebab-case conversion happens automatically.

question: What is the difference between stopPropagation() and stopImmediatePropagation() in event propagation?
options: stopPropagation() only stops capturing, and stopImmediatePropagation() only stops bubbling
Both methods work identically
stopPropagation() also prevents the default action, but stopImmediatePropagation() does not
stopImmediatePropagation() also stops the remaining listeners on the same element from executing
explanation: stopPropagation() prevents the event from propagating to parent/child elements, but other listeners registered on the same element will still execute. stopImmediatePropagation() prevents propagation and also stops the remaining listeners on the same element from executing. Preventing the default action is handled by preventDefault().

question: What is the output order of the following code?
options: parent capture, child capture, child bubble, parent bubble
child capture, child bubble, parent capture, parent bubble
parent bubble, child bubble, child capture, parent capture
child bubble, parent bubble, child capture, parent capture
explanation: DOM events propagate in the order: capturing -> target -> bubbling. First, during the capturing phase, 'parent capture' executes. Then, during the target phase, the listeners on the child execute in registration order: 'child capture', 'child bubble'. Finally, during the bubbling phase, 'parent bubble' executes. On the target element, registration order takes precedence over the capture/bubble distinction.

question: What is the correct way to change an element's inline style with JavaScript?
options: element.style.backgroundColor = 'red'
element.css('background-color', 'red')
element.setStyle('background-color', 'red')
element.style['background-color'] = 'red'
explanation: Inline styles can be set through the element.style object. CSS property names are converted to camelCase (background-color becomes backgroundColor). While element.style['background-color'] also works, camelCase is the standard approach.

question: What is the difference between cloneNode(true) and cloneNode(false)?
options: cloneNode(true) also copies event listeners
cloneNode(false) copies child nodes as well
cloneNode(true) performs a deep clone including all child nodes
cloneNode(false) removes the original node
explanation: cloneNode(true) performs a deep clone of the node and all its descendant nodes. cloneNode(false) copies only the node itself without its children. In both cases, event listeners registered with addEventListener() are not copied. Inline event handlers (onclick attributes) are copied.

question: What is the DOM structure after running the following code?
options: a, b, c
c, a, b
a, c, b
c, b, a
explanation: append(a, b) adds elements in the order a, b. insertBefore(c, a) inserts c before a. Therefore, the final order is c, a, b. In insertBefore(newNode, referenceNode), newNode is inserted before referenceNode.

question: What is the correct purpose of MutationObserver?
options: It monitors changes in network requests
It monitors value changes of JavaScript variables
It synchronously monitors CSS style changes
It asynchronously monitors changes to the DOM tree
explanation: MutationObserver is an API that asynchronously monitors changes to the DOM tree (adding/removing child nodes, attribute changes, text changes, etc.). Callbacks are executed in the microtask queue, allowing multiple DOM changes to be processed in batches. It replaces the older synchronous Mutation Events.

question: Why does the click event not work in the following code?
options: Because the handler function is used before its declaration
Because the function's return value (undefined) is passed as the second argument to addEventListener
Because getElementById returns null
Because the click event name is incorrect
explanation: handler() immediately invokes the function and passes its return value (undefined) to addEventListener. To pass a function reference, you should use handler (without parentheses). This is a common mistake in JavaScript, and it's important to understand the difference between a function reference and a function call.

question: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: childNodes returns all child nodes including text nodes. In ' hello ', there are 3 childNodes: a leading whitespace text node, the span element, and a trailing whitespace text node. children returns only Element nodes, so it includes only the 1 span.

question: What are the coordinates returned by getBoundingClientRect() relative to?
options: The top-left corner of the entire document
The top-left corner of the parent element
The top-left corner of the viewport
The top-left corner of the body element
explanation: getBoundingClientRect() returns the position and size of an element relative to the top-left corner of the viewport (the visible area of the browser). Since scroll position is factored in, you need to add window.scrollX/scrollY to get document-relative coordinates. It has top, right, bottom, left, width, and height properties.

question: What is the problem with the following code?
options: querySelectorAll does not support forEach
You cannot assign a number directly to style.order — it must be a string
The index parameter cannot be used in forEach
There is no problem — the code works correctly
explanation: This code works correctly. NodeList supports forEach(), and when a number is assigned to a style property, it is automatically converted to a string. CSS properties without units (order, z-index, opacity, etc.) can have numbers assigned directly. querySelectorAll returns a static NodeList.

question: What is the correct behavior of the element.closest() method?
options: It returns the nearest matching element among child elements
Starting from itself, it traverses up the ancestor chain and returns the nearest element matching the CSS selector
It returns the nearest matching element among sibling elements
It returns the nearest matching element in the entire document
explanation: closest() starts from the element itself and traverses up through parent, grandparent, and so on, returning the first element that matches the CSS selector. If the element itself matches, it returns itself. If no matching element is found, it returns null. This is particularly useful for finding a specific parent element during event delegation.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options: true, true
false, false
true, false
false, true
explanation: The contains() method checks whether the node it is called on contains the given node as a descendant. Since div has p as a child, div.contains(p) is true. Conversely, p does not contain div, so p.contains(div) is false. Note that contains() also returns true when called on itself (node.contains(node) is true).

question: Why doesn't the following code work as intended?
options: NodeList returned by querySelectorAll does not support index access
var should be used instead of let
classList.toggle is not a valid method
There is no problem — using let correctly captures i for each iteration
explanation: This code works correctly. Variables declared with let have block scope, so a new binding is created for each iteration. Therefore, the i inside the click handler correctly references the right index. If var had been used instead, all handlers would reference the last index value due to closure behavior.

question: What is the difference between the DOMContentLoaded event and the load event?
options: DOMContentLoaded fires after HTML parsing is complete, while load fires after all resources are loaded
load always fires before DOMContentLoaded
Both events fire at the same time
DOMContentLoaded fires after images have finished loading
explanation: DOMContentLoaded fires as soon as the HTML document has been fully parsed and the DOM tree has been constructed. It does not wait for external resources like stylesheets, images, or subframes to finish loading. The load event fires after all resources (images, stylesheets, scripts, etc.) have been completely loaded. Therefore, DOMContentLoaded always fires first.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options: World, [object HTMLDivElement]
Hello, null
Hello, [object HTMLDivElement]
TypeError: Cannot read properties of null
explanation: Reassigning innerHTML removes all existing child nodes and replaces them with new ones. The previously referenced p element is detached from the DOM tree, but since a JavaScript variable still holds a reference to it, it is not garbage collected. p.textContent is still 'Hello', but parentNode becomes null since it is no longer part of any DOM tree.

question: What is the correct description of the element.remove() method?
options: It removes all child elements
It removes the element from the DOM tree
It removes all attributes from the element
It removes all event listeners from the element
explanation: element.remove() removes the element from its parent node. Previously, you had to use parentNode.removeChild(element), but remove() allows you to directly remove the element without needing a parent reference, resulting in more concise code. The removed element remains in memory and can be reinserted.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options:
• 1
  • 2
  • 3
Empty string
Infinite loop occurs
explanation:
getElementsByTagName() returns a live HTMLCollection. When items[0].remove() removes the first li, the collection automatically updates and items.length decreases. The while loop continues as long as items.length > 0, so all li elements are removed. The ul element remains, resulting in '
    ' being output.

question: Why does getComputedStyle not return the expected value in the following code?
options: Because getComputedStyle cannot read inline styles
Because getPropertyValue('width') should be used instead of .width
Because the element is not attached to the DOM, so the browser does not compute the layout
Because a selector string should be passed to getComputedStyle instead of an element
explanation: getComputedStyle() returns the style values that the browser has actually computed. If the element has not been added to the DOM tree, the browser does not perform layout calculations, so the computed value is an empty string. After adding it to the DOM with document.body.appendChild(div), calling getComputedStyle would return '100px'.

question: What is the difference between outerHTML and innerHTML?
options: outerHTML returns the HTML including the element itself, while innerHTML returns only the children's HTML
innerHTML includes the element itself, while outerHTML returns only the children
Both properties return the same result
outerHTML is read-only and cannot be modified
explanation:
innerHTML returns the child nodes of an element as an HTML string. outerHTML returns the complete HTML including the element itself. For example, for
hi
, div.innerHTML is 'hi' while div.outerHTML is '
hi
'. Assigning a value to outerHTML replaces the element itself.

question: What is the correct reason for using requestAnimationFrame for DOM manipulation?
options: To apply DOM changes synchronously and immediately
Because it always executes before setTimeout(fn, 0)
To execute the callback just before the browser's next repaint for smooth animations
Because it runs in the microtask queue and has higher priority than other tasks
explanation: requestAnimationFrame (rAF) executes its callback just before the browser performs the next repaint. It is typically called 60 times per second (60fps) and is optimized to match the display's refresh rate. Unlike setTimeout, the browser pauses rAF calls in inactive tabs to conserve resources. Performing DOM animations and layout changes inside rAF enables smooth rendering without frame drops.

question: Which of the following is NOT a valid position value for the first argument of insertAdjacentHTML()?
options: beforebegin
afterbegin
innerend
afterend
explanation: insertAdjacentHTML() supports 4 positions: 'beforebegin' (before the element), 'afterbegin' (before the first child), 'beforeend' (after the last child), and 'afterend' (after the element). 'innerend' is not a valid position value. Unlike innerHTML, it inserts HTML without destroying the existing DOM, which is better for performance.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options: SPAN, [object HTMLDivElement]
P, null
P, [object HTMLDivElement]
SPAN, null
explanation: replaceWith() replaces the element it is called on with the given node(s). Since span was replaced by p, the first child of div is now p. The replaced span is detached from the DOM, so its parentNode becomes null. replaceWith() can also accept multiple nodes and strings as arguments.

question: What is the role of the element.matches() method?
options: It returns a boolean indicating whether the element matches the given CSS selector
It returns child elements that match the CSS selector
It returns all matching elements as an array
It compares whether two elements are the same node
explanation: matches() checks whether the element matches the given CSS selector and returns true or false. It is particularly useful in event delegation to check whether event.target is a specific element. For example, event.target.matches('.btn') checks whether the clicked element has the .btn class.

question: Why is the content inside the template not displayed on screen in the following code?
options: The content of a template tag is not rendered, and must be accessed via content.children instead of children
Template tags cannot be accessed from JavaScript
Templates created via innerHTML are ignored
Div elements cannot be placed inside a template
explanation: The content of an HTML <template> element is inert and not rendered. Its internal content is accessed through the content property, which is a DocumentFragment. tmpl.children.length is 0, but tmpl.content.children.length is 1. To use it, clone it with document.importNode(tmpl.content, true) and then insert it into the DOM.

question: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: The after() method inserts nodes right after the element it is called on. li1.after(li2) inserts li2 right after li1. Therefore, ul has two children (li1 and li2), and the last element child li2 has textContent 'B'. The before() method works in the opposite direction, inserting before the element.

question: What is the correct difference between offsetWidth and getBoundingClientRect().width?
options: offsetWidth includes decimal points, while getBoundingClientRect().width returns only integers
getBoundingClientRect().width does not reflect CSS transforms, but offsetWidth does
offsetWidth returns a rounded integer value, while getBoundingClientRect().width returns a precise floating-point value
Both values are always identical
explanation: offsetWidth returns the element's layout width as a rounded integer (including border and padding). getBoundingClientRect().width returns a precise floating-point value and reflects the actual rendered size after CSS transforms (such as scale) are applied. Use getBoundingClientRect() when precise size calculations are needed.

question: Why doesn't the following code remove all children as intended?
options: The remove() method cannot be called on children elements
children is a live collection, so removing elements shifts indices and causes some elements to be skipped
const should be used instead of let
Elements created via innerHTML cannot be removed with remove()
explanation: children is a live HTMLCollection. When children[0] is removed, the previous children[1] becomes children[0] and the length decreases. But i increments to 1, so it processes the new children[1] (originally children[2]), skipping the middle element. Solutions: use while (parent.firstChild) parent.firstChild.remove(), or create a static array with [...children] before iterating.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options:
one

two

three

three

one

one

three
two
explanation: replaceChildren() removes all existing child nodes and replaces them with the nodes passed as arguments. ps[2] is 'three' and ps[0] is 'one', so they are inserted in that order. Since querySelectorAll returns a static NodeList, the references to ps[0] and ps[2] remain valid despite DOM changes.

question: What does element.scrollIntoView({ behavior: 'smooth' }) do?
options: It removes the element from the DOM and reinserts it at the top of the viewport
It scrolls with a smooth animation so the element becomes visible in the viewport
It changes the element's CSS position to fixed
It resizes the viewport to fit the element
explanation: scrollIntoView() scrolls the page so that the element becomes visible to the user. The behavior: 'smooth' option applies a smooth scrolling animation instead of an instant jump. The block and inline options can also be used to specify vertical and horizontal alignment positions. It does not modify the DOM structure or CSS.

question: What is the correct difference between createTreeWalker() and querySelectorAll()?
options: querySelectorAll() returns a live collection, while createTreeWalker() returns a static snapshot
createTreeWalker() can traverse all node types including text and comment nodes, while querySelectorAll() returns only Element nodes
createTreeWalker() supports CSS selectors, while querySelectorAll() does not
Both APIs return the same results
explanation: createTreeWalker() is a low-level API that can traverse various node types using NodeFilter, including text nodes (NodeFilter.SHOW_TEXT) and comment nodes (NodeFilter.SHOW_COMMENT). querySelectorAll() returns only Element nodes matching CSS selectors as a static NodeList. TreeWalker can define complex traversal conditions using custom filter functions, making it useful for fine-grained DOM tree exploration.

question: What is the correct key characteristic of Shadow DOM?
options: Shadow DOM creates an independent DOM tree that encapsulates styles and markup, unaffected by external CSS
Shadow DOM is a completely isolated DOM that cannot be accessed from JavaScript
Shadow DOM can only be used with server-side rendering
Shadow DOM works in the same way as an iframe
explanation: Shadow DOM is a core technology of Web Components that creates an encapsulated DOM tree inside a host element. External CSS does not affect the Shadow DOM's interior, and internal styles do not leak to the outside. It is created with element.attachShadow({ mode: 'open' }), and when mode is 'open', it can be accessed via element.shadowRoot; when 'closed', it returns null.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options: true, true, false
false, true, false
false, false, true
undefined, true, false
explanation: isConnected is a read-only boolean property that indicates whether a node is currently connected to the document's DOM tree. A node created with createElement is not yet connected, so it returns false. After being added with appendChild, it becomes true. After being removed with remove(), it returns false again.

question: What is the correct purpose of IntersectionObserver?
options: It detects when the style properties of two DOM elements overlap
It detects event conflicts between elements
It checks whether the z-index of two elements overlap
It asynchronously observes the ratio at which a target element intersects with the viewport or a specified ancestor element
explanation: IntersectionObserver asynchronously detects when a target element enters or exits the visible area of the viewport or a specified root element. It is used for infinite scrolling, lazy loading of images, and ad visibility tracking. Unlike scroll event listeners, it does not block the main thread, making it more performant.

question: Why does toggleAttribute behave differently than expected in the following code?
options: The second argument true forces addition, and the subsequent calls toggle, so disabled ends up present
toggleAttribute can only be used with boolean attributes, causing an error
When toggleAttribute is called consecutively, only the last call takes effect
toggleAttribute works the same as setAttribute
explanation: In toggleAttribute(name, force), when force is true, the attribute is forcibly added; when false, it is forcibly removed. When force is omitted, it toggles the attribute's presence. The first call adds disabled (force=true), the second toggles it off (removed), and the third toggles it back on (added). Therefore, the final result is true.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options: A, B
B, B
A, A
B, A
explanation: append() adds a node after the parent's last child, while prepend() inserts a node before the parent's first child. After appending li1 ('A') and then prepending li2 ('B'), the order becomes li2, li1. Therefore, firstElementChild is 'B' and lastElementChild is 'A'.

question: What is the correct difference between adoptNode() and importNode()?
options: adoptNode() copies the node, while importNode() moves the original
adoptNode() moves the original node to the current document, while importNode() copies the node into the current document
Both methods work identically
importNode() copies event listeners as well, but adoptNode() does not
explanation: adoptNode() moves a node from another document to the current document. The node is removed from the original document and its ownerDocument is changed. importNode() copies (clones) a node into the current document while leaving the original intact. In both cases, the returned node must be explicitly inserted into the DOM using methods like appendChild.

question: What is the output of the following code?
options: 1, Hello World
3, Hello
3, Hello World
1, Hello
explanation: When strings are passed to append(), each becomes a separate Text node. Three text nodes are created: 'Hello', ' ', and 'World'. The normalize() method merges adjacent text nodes into one and removes empty text nodes. Therefore, the three text nodes are merged into 'Hello World', resulting in childNodes.length of 1.

question: What is the correct purpose of ResizeObserver?
options: It only detects changes in the browser window size
It detects changes in CSS media queries
It detects when images have finished loading
It asynchronously detects size changes of individual DOM elements
explanation: ResizeObserver asynchronously detects changes in the content area or border box size of individual DOM elements. Unlike the window resize event, it can directly observe size changes of specific elements, making it useful for implementing container queries and responsive components. The callback receives an array of ResizeObserverEntry objects.

question: Why does text extraction using Range fail in the following code?
options: createRange() only works on elements connected to the DOM
selectNode should be used instead of selectNodeContents
There is no problem — 'Hello World' is output correctly
range.toString() returns a string that includes HTML tags
explanation: This code works correctly. Range objects can be used even on elements not connected to the DOM. selectNodeContents() includes all children of the node in the range, and toString() returns only the text content within the range. HTML tags are not included, so 'Hello World' is output.

question: What is the correct difference between previousElementSibling and previousSibling?
options: previousSibling returns only Element nodes, while previousElementSibling returns all node types
Both properties return the same node
previousElementSibling returns the parent element
previousElementSibling returns only Element nodes, while previousSibling can also return text or comment nodes
explanation: previousSibling returns the previous sibling node, which can be any node type including text nodes, comment nodes, and Element nodes. previousElementSibling returns only the previous sibling that is an Element node. Since line breaks and whitespace in HTML create text nodes, it is safer to use previousElementSibling when traversing only elements.
